title: Lesson 4 — De Morgan's Law
_authoring: Pedagogical full pass (curriculum audit). Added intro with lessonIntro (explicitly bridges from L3: NAND = NOT(A AND B) is WHY De Morgan works), per-KC explanations (de_morgan with BOTH law forms stated explicitly, negation for NOR/complement forms), and a worked example (apply De Morgan to NOT(A AND B) showing the flip-negation-AND-flip-operator steps). Reordered items: clean application items first — l4-nand-equiv (tier 1) and l4-nor-equiv (tier 1) moved to FIRST two positions; trap items l4-nand2-trap and l4-nor2-trap moved to after clean introductory items; difficultyTier adjusted so clean intro items are tier 1 and the trap items that formerly were tier 1 are now tier 2. This teaches the law before presenting its traps. All truthTables preserved from original (verified via @polymath/booleans).

lessonIntro.title: Lesson 4 — De Morgan's Law
lessonIntro.body: In Lesson 3 you built everything from NAND — and NAND is NOT(A AND B). De Morgan's law is WHY that works: it says that negating a compound expression flips each negation AND flips the connective. Two forms: NOT(A AND B) = (NOT A) OR (NOT B), and NOT(A OR B) = (NOT A) AND (NOT B). Every half-correct application misses one of those two flips — the items in this lesson are designed to catch exactly that mistake.

explanations.body: De Morgan's law comes in two symmetric forms. Form 1: NOT(A AND B) = (NOT A) OR (NOT B). The negation distributes inward, AND flips to OR. Form 2: NOT(A OR B) = (NOT A) AND (NOT B). The negation distributes inward, OR flips to AND. The critical rule: when the NOT goes in, the connective MUST flip — AND becomes OR, OR becomes AND. Negating the variables without flipping the connective is the most common mistake.

NOR (Not-OR) is NOT(A OR B). Its truth table [1,0,0,0] shows it is true only when every input is false. NOR is also a universal gate (like NAND) — it can build every Boolean function. The De Morgan equivalent of NOR is (NOT A) AND (NOT B): the NOR of two values equals the AND of their complements.

steps.label: Identify the outer connective
steps.detail: The expression is NOT applied to (A AND B). The outer connective inside the negation is AND.

steps.label: Apply De Morgan Form 1
steps.detail: De Morgan says: NOT(A AND B) = (NOT A) OR (NOT B). Two things happen simultaneously: each variable gets negated, AND the AND flips to OR. Do both steps — if you only negate the variables but keep AND, you get (NOT A) AND (NOT B), which is the NOR table, not the NAND table.

steps.label: Verify: compute (NOT A) OR (NOT B) row by row
Cross-check: compute NOT(A AND B) directly
steps.detail: NOT(0 AND 0)=NOT 0=1, NOT(0 AND 1)=NOT 0=1, NOT(1 AND 0)=NOT 0=1, NOT(1 AND 1)=NOT 1=0. Same table [1,1,1,0] — De Morgan's equivalence confirmed.
